item.title: Microsoft's MAI Models Are Quietly Replacing OpenAI Inside Office 365 — and Big Tech's In-House AI Race Just Sped Up
item.body:
For years, the AI features inside Microsoft Office carried an unspoken asterisk: much of the intelligence behind them came from somewhere else. Copilot's drafting, summarizing, and formula help leaned heavily on OpenAI's models, with Anthropic's models reportedly added to the mix more recently. That arrangement is now visibly changing. According to recent reporting, Microsoft has begun routing a growing share of AI requests in Excel, Outlook, and other Office 365 products to its own MAI family of models — work that already handles tens of thousands of prompts every week that previously went to third-party systems.

It's a quiet switch most users will never see a notification about. But it may be one of the most consequential shifts in the AI industry this year.

## What Actually Changed

Microsoft AI (MAI) is the in-house model division led by Mustafa Suleyman, the DeepMind co-founder who joined Microsoft in 2024. The team has been building its own foundation models — including text models trained on Microsoft's massive GPU clusters — with the explicit goal of reducing the company's dependence on outside providers.

The recent change is about deployment, not just research. Reports indicate that Microsoft has started substituting MAI models for OpenAI and Anthropic models in real production workloads across Office 365:

- **Excel**: assistance with formulas, data explanations, and table analysis
- **Outlook**: email drafting, thread summarization, and reply suggestions
- **Other Office surfaces**: document summarization and routine writing tasks across the suite

The volumes cited — tens of thousands of weekly prompts — are still a small fraction of Copilot's total traffic. But the direction matters more than the current share. Microsoft is treating its own models as production-ready for everyday office work, and it is expanding their footprint feature by feature.

## Why Microsoft Is Doing This

Several forces are pushing Microsoft toward AI self-sufficiency, and none of them are going away.

**1. Cost at scale.** Every Copilot request that hits a partner's model carries a cost, whether in compute-sharing arrangements or opportunity cost on Microsoft's own infrastructure. When you serve hundreds of millions of Office users, even small per-request savings compound into enormous numbers. Models that Microsoft owns outright, optimized for its own Azure hardware, are simply cheaper to run for routine tasks.

**2. A changed relationship with OpenAI.** The Microsoft–OpenAI partnership was restructured in late 2025, giving both sides more independence. OpenAI can now work more freely with other cloud providers, and Microsoft, in turn, has stronger incentives to stand on its own feet. Analysts have long noted that Microsoft never wanted to be permanently dependent on a single external lab for the intelligence layer of its most important products.

**3. Fit-for-purpose beats frontier.** Not every task needs the most powerful model in the world. Summarizing an email thread or explaining a spreadsheet formula is well within reach of smaller, cheaper models — and a model tuned specifically for Office workflows can sometimes do the job better than a general-purpose frontier system. Industry researchers often describe this as the "right-sizing" of AI: matching the model to the task instead of sending everything to the largest model available.

**4. Control and speed.** Owning the full stack — chips, data centers, models, and applications — lets Microsoft ship features on its own schedule, tune behavior for enterprise compliance needs, and respond to issues without waiting on a partner's roadmap.

## The Bigger Pattern: Everyone Is Going In-House

Microsoft's move is part of a broader realignment across the technology industry.

- **Google** builds Gemini in-house and threads it through Workspace, Android, and Search.
- **Meta** develops its Llama family and uses it across its apps rather than licensing external models.
- **Amazon** introduced its Nova models to power features across its own services while investing in outside labs as a hedge.
- **Apple** has pursued a hybrid approach, combining its own foundation models with selective partnerships.

The logic is consistent everywhere: AI is becoming core infrastructure, and the biggest platform companies do not want core infrastructure they don't control. In the 2010s, the equivalent battle was over custom silicon and cloud data centers. In the 2020s, it's foundation models.

For OpenAI and Anthropic, this doesn't mean the partnerships are over — frontier models still power the most demanding tasks, and licensing deals remain lucrative. But it does mean the labs' biggest distribution partners are also becoming their competitors, a tension that will shape pricing, product strategy, and future negotiations across the industry.

## What This Means for Office Users

For most people, the transition should be invisible by design. Microsoft is unlikely to swap models in places where quality would visibly drop; the whole point of a gradual rollout is to move traffic only where its own models perform comparably.

Still, there are a few practical implications worth knowing:

- **Feature behavior may shift subtly.** Different models have different writing styles and strengths. If Copilot's email drafts or summaries feel slightly different over time, a model change may be why.
- **Expect faster, cheaper AI features.** In-house models running on optimized infrastructure can reduce latency and lower the cost of offering AI broadly — which could eventually influence how Copilot is priced and packaged.
- **Enterprise customers gain a clearer accountability chain.** When Microsoft owns the model, questions about data handling, compliance, and behavior go to one company instead of two.

## What to Watch Next

Three signals will show how fast this transition is really moving. First, watch whether Microsoft names MAI models explicitly in Copilot documentation and enterprise controls — public model choice would signal confidence. Second, watch the share of Copilot workloads that shift in-house; tens of thousands of weekly prompts is a pilot, but millions would be a strategy fully realized. Third, watch how OpenAI responds, particularly in its own productivity-adjacent products and its relationships with other cloud providers.

The AI era's first phase was defined by partnerships: platform giants bolting cutting-edge models from specialized labs onto their products. The second phase, now underway, looks more like the rest of tech history — vertical integration, in-house control, and a handful of companies determined to own every layer of the stack. Microsoft's quiet model swap inside Office 365 may be remembered as the moment that second phase became impossible to ignore.
